coredata.dc:title: Selective sensing of heavy metal ions via fluorescence quenching of femtosecond-laser-synthesized 2D nanoparticles
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Shahin, Ahmed
Ibrahim, Khaled
Ye, Fan
Karimi, Reza
Sanderson, Joseph
Musselman, Kevin P.
coredata.dc:description: A femtosecond laser is used to break and exfoliate two dimensional (2D) flakes of nitrogen-doped graphene oxide (NGO) or molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) dispersed in acetonitrile or a 1:1 ethanol-water mixture and convert them into nanoparticles (NPs) in 70–100 min. The chemically active NPs bond with atoms from the laser-dissociated acetonitrile or ethanol and water molecules to form 2D NPs functionalized with varying quantities of nitrogen, oxygen, hydrogen, and carbon groups. Sensitive and selective detection of heavy metal ions (HMI) Hg2+, As3+, Pb2+, and Cd2+ is demonstrated with the solvent-functionalized NGO and MoS2 NPs. Binding between the HMI and functional groups leads to quenching of the NPs’ photoluminescence. Limits of detection of 3 nm (Hg2+), 3.8 nm (As3+), 1.8 nm (Pb2+), and 13 nm (Cd2+) are demonstrated with the solvent-functionalized NGO in acetonitrile, NGO in acetonitrile, MoS2 in acetonitrile, and NGO in ethanol-water, respectively. The selectivity is attributed to varying bond polarities associated with the different combinations of NPs and functional groups, which electrostatically attract HMI with different affinities. The functional groups responsible for the selective sensing in the different NP solutions are identified using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. This rapid, one-pot, room-temperature process can be used to tailor 2D materials for targeted chemical sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Two-dimensional materials
Heavy metal ions
Fluorescence Sensing
Femtosecond laser treatment

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fs laser treatment of 2D stacks of NGO in ethanol-water mixture stirred at 200 rpm. The laser breaks the NGO flakes into smaller NPs and exfoliates them. Dissociated ethanol and water molecules bond with chemically active NGO NPs to tune the properties of the NPs.

TEM images of the MoS2 NPs formed by laser treatment in acetonitrile for 70 min. (a) Average NP size of approximately 7–8 nm. (b) Different lattice spacings owing to the addition of different functional groups.

Raman spectra of (a) NGO NPs laser treated in ethanol-water and untreated bulk NGO flakes and (b) NGO NPs laser treated in acetonitrile. (c)-(d) MoS2 NPs laser treated in acetonitrile and untreated bulk MoS2 powder.

Deconvoluted XPS spectra of laser-treated NPs. (a) C 1s of NGO in acetonitrile, (b) N 1s of NGO in acetonitrile, (c) C 1s of NGO in ethanol-water, (d) O 1s of NGO in ethanol-water, (e) Mo 3d, (f) S 2p, and (g) N 1s spectra of MoS2 in acetonitrile.
Normalized absorption spectra of (a) NGO NPs in ethanol-water, (b) NGO NPs in acetonitrile, and (c) MoS2 NPs in acetonitrile.
PL spectra of (a) NGO NPs in ethanol-water, (b) NGO NPs in acetonitrile, and (c) MoS2 NPs in acetonitrile.
Fluorescence spectra of NGO NPs sensing (a) Hg2+ and (d) As3+, with corresponding calibration curve (b,e) plots, and (c,f) fittings.
Cd2+ sensing: (a) fluorescence quenching, calibration curve (b) plot, and (c) fitting.
Fluorescence spectra of MoS2 NPs sensing (a) Pb2+ and (d) Hg2+, with corresponding calibration curve (b,e) plots, and (c,f) fittings.

Quenching factor bar graphs for laser-treated NPs: (a) MoS2 in acetonitrile, (b) NGO in ethanol-water, and (c) NGO in acetonitrile. The asterisk (*) indicates combinations where reproducible quenching behavior was not observed. The red bars indicate stable PL quenching, and black bars indicate limited quenching. The values on the graphs indicate the concentrations at which the quenching factors were measured.

PL quenching due to the interaction of Hg2+ with the negatively charged nitrogen and hydroxyl functional groups of the NGO and MoS2 NPs laser-treated in acetonitrile. HMI interaction with NGO and MoS2 NPs treated in the same solvent leads to different PL quenching due to the difference in bond polarities of the NPs/functional groups.

FTIR spectra of (a) NGO NPs in acetonitrile without (black curve) and with (red curve) the addition of As3+, (b) NGO NPs in acetonitrile without and with Hg2+, (c) MoS2 NPs in acetonitrile without and with Pb2+, (d) and MoS2 NPs in acetonitrile with and without Hg2+.
PL lifetime measurements of NGO laser treated in ethanol-water with (blue) and without (red) the addition of 10 µm of Cd2+. The instrument response function (IRF) is shown in black.
Materials and laser treatment times used in this work.
Summary of sensing results and the functional groups responsible for sensing.

row.entry: C – H, N – H, O – H

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Summary of current state of the art in comparison with this work.

row.entry: Binding energy change based on functionalization
Trapping of Hg in the nitrogen defect centers in carbon

entry.bold: Fs laser treatment for 70 and 100 mins

head.ce:title: Selective sensing of heavy metal ions via fluorescence quenching of femtosecond-laser-synthesized 2D nanoparticles

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Waterloo Institute of Nanotechnology, University of Waterloo

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A femtosecond laser is used to break and exfoliate two dimensional (2D) flakes of nitrogen-doped graphene oxide (NGO) or molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) dispersed in acetonitrile or a 1:1 ethanol-water mixture and convert them into nanoparticles (NPs) in 70–100 min. The chemically active NPs bond with atoms from the laser-dissociated acetonitrile or ethanol and water molecules to form 2D NPs functionalized with varying quantities of nitrogen, oxygen, hydrogen, and carbon groups. Sensitive and selective detection of heavy metal ions (HMI) Hg2+, As3+, Pb2+, and Cd2+ is demonstrated with the solvent-functionalized NGO and MoS2 NPs. Binding between the HMI and functional groups leads to quenching of the NPs’ photoluminescence. Limits of detection of 3 nm (Hg2+), 3.8 nm (As3+), 1.8 nm (Pb2+), and 13 nm (Cd2+) are demonstrated with the solvent-functionalized NGO in acetonitrile, NGO in acetonitrile, MoS2 in acetonitrile, and NGO in ethanol-water, respectively. The selectivity is attributed to varying bond polarities associated with the different combinations of NPs and functional groups, which electrostatically attract HMI with different affinities. The functional groups responsible for the selective sensing in the different NP solutions are identified using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. This rapid, one-pot, room-temperature process can be used to tailor 2D materials for targeted chemical sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: The World Health Organization (WHO) has identified mercury, arsenic, lead, and cadmium as toxic heavy metal ions (HMI) of serious concern, as they represent major threats to many aquatic and human lives [1]. Irrigation water contaminated with HMI affects the soil productivity and can be toxic to many plants at very low HMI concentrations [2]. HMI do not degrade, which makes them capable of disrupting the human central nervous system’s functionality. They accumulate in human fatty tissues, so even very low concentrations of HMI can accumulate gradually and affect human health [3]. Techniques such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy and optical mass spectroscopy can be used to detect HMI, but they are laboratory methods [4]. The fabrication of chemical sensors capable of early-stage, on-site detection of low concentrations of HMI in water is of great necessity. Two-dimensional (2D) materials are widely used in sensing applications for their high surface-area-to-volume ratio [5,6]. Furthermore, quantum confinement of 2D materials, particularly 2D quantum dots (QDs), can yield high absorption and fluorescence [7]. Due to their superb morphological, optical, chemical, and electronic properties, 2D nanoparticles (NPs) are strong candidates for fluorescence-based, on-site detection of HMI with very high sensitivities of a few nanomolars (nm).

2D materials like MoS2, black phosphorus (BP), and graphene in the form of nanosheets and QDs are being researched extensively for sensing applications including biosensing [8,9], gas sensing [10], and humidity sensing, [11]. The defective nature of the 2D materials can help in sensing HMI. For example, graphitic carbon nitride QDs were used to sense Hg ions via fluorescence quenching upon trapping Hg in the nitrogen defect sites [12]. Functionalization of 2D materials can also enable HMI sensing. MoS2 nanosheets functionalized with an aptamer detected As ions by quenching of the nanosheets’ fluorescence [5]. The chemical affinity between HMI and functional groups, like oxides, enhances their bonding and allows sensitive HMI detection [4]. Carbon QDs were synthesized to sense Pb and As ions based on the affinity of functional groups to bind with the HMI [13]. The ability to tune 2D NPs by modifying their surfaces with different functional groups allows them to sense various HMI selectively [14]. Surface-functionalized MoS2 QDs were utilized to selectively detect Co, Cd, and Pb, where bonding of the HMI and functional groups resulted in a binding-energy change that turned on the fluorescence of the QDs [15].

Despite these encouraging results, the area of HMI fluorescence sensing using 2D NPs is still not fully explored. Few studies have considered fluorescence sensing of cadmium in water (most papers have focused on detecting lead and mercury) and many combinations of 2D materials and HMI have yet to be studied (e.g., MoS2 NPs have not been used for sensing Hg ions). Most notably, the functionalization of 2D NPs with different functional groups to detect the four most dangerous HMI (Hg, As, Cd, Pb) using the same synthesis and sensing techniques is yet to be demonstrated.

In this work, 2D NP synthesis and functionalization are simultaneously achieved using a femtosecond (fs) laser treatment process. The fs laser treatment is a one-pot, room-temperature process that takes only 70–100 mins. MoS2 and nitrogen-doped graphene oxide (NGO) powders are individually laser treated in acetonitrile or a water-ethanol mixture. The fs laser breaks the flakes into 2D NPs and solvent-derived radicals bond to the periphery of the 2D NPs. This flexible functionalization technique enhances the sensitivity and selectivity of the 2D NPs to HMI without complex fabrication steps. Sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+, As3+, Pb2+, and Cd2+ ions based on fluorescence quenching is demonstrated.

MoS2 ultrafine powder (average particle size ~90 nm and 99% purity) and NGO nanoplatelets were purchased from Graphene Supermarket. Acetonitrile (ReagentPlus 99%, Sigma Aldrich), pure ethanol and deionized water were used as solvents.

Powders were dispersed individually in the solvents to produce 0.1 mg/mL solutions. MoS2 was dispersed in acetonitrile and NGO was dispersed in either acetonitrile or an ethanol-water mixture (50% v/v), as listed in Table 1. The solutions were sonicated for 10 mins to prevent agglomerations and ensure solution homogeneity, then 4 mL of each solution was pipetted into a glass vial for laser treatment. For the sensing experiments, metal trace standard aqueous solutions were purchased from Inorganic Ventures: lead (0.1 mg/mL, 0.5% v/v HNO3), mercury (0.1 mg/mL, 7% v/v HNO3), cadmium (0.1 mg/mL, 3% v/v HNO3), and arsenic (0.01 mg/mL, 2% v/v HNO3). The solutions were further diluted down to concentration of 0.1 µm - 50 µm with their respective nitric acid concentrations for sensing trials.

A fs laser can be used to alter the properties or size of a material [16,17]. Laser treatment can break and exfoliate bulk 2D materials into smaller-sized sheets if the fluence of the laser is higher than the ablation threshold of the bulk material [18]. The laser’s strong electric field can remove the electrons responsible for holding the 2D sheets in stacks, forming positive clusters. This leads to Coulomb explosion due to the repulsion of the atomic charges and causes exfoliation of the 2D stacks [19]. Further material size reduction is possible at longer irradiation times where the high electric field near the energetic laser focal point breaks the 2D material into smaller particles [19]. In this work, a Spitfire Ace-35 F chirped pulse amplification system was used for all laser treatments and it produced ultrafast laser pulses centered at 800 nm wavelength, 35 fs pulse duration, 1 cm beam diameter, and 1 kHz repetition rate. The average power of the pulses was fixed at 2 W. The solutions were stirred using a magnetic stirrer at 200 rpm during the treatment process to maintain the homogeneity of the solutions. The solutions were exposed to the laser beam for the treatment times shown in Table 1. The fs laser was focused using a plano-convex focal lens (5 cm focal length) where the focal point was 1 cm below the solvent level, as shown in the schematic in Fig. 1.

The laser-treated NP solutions were filtered using a 0.22 µm filter to remove any untreated agglomerations for all characterization tests. A Horiba QuantaMaster 8000 was used to obtain the absorption spectra, photoluminescence (PL) spectra, and PL lifetime decay of the NP solutions. A 350 nm NanoLED was used to excite the NP solutions for PL lifetime decay measurements. The instrument response function (IRF) of the PL lifetime decay measurements was measured using colloidal silica. A window and two glass slides were used for Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) sample preparation, where 100 µL of NP solutions, with and without added HMI, were drop casted on one glass slide and the other slide was placed on top of the solution-containing-slide. The two slides were placed in the FTIR window inserted in a SHIMADZU FTIR-8400S spectrometer. For Raman and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), untreated solutions and 100 µL of the laser-treated and filtered solutions were drop casted on diced and cleaned silicon substrates. A Renishaw micro-Raman was used to obtain the Raman spectra of the solutions using a wavelength of 632.8 nm and a power of 20 mW. XPS measurements were performed using a Thermo-VG Scientific ESCALab 250 microprobe and a 1486.6 eV monochromatic aluminum Kα source. For transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization, NP solutions were drop casted on lacey carbon grids and filter paper was placed beneath the grid to absorb the solvent. The TEM images were collected using a Zeiss Libra 200 MC operating at 200 kV.

The PL spectra of blank NP solutions (without HMI added) were recorded several times to account for the sensitivity of the spectrometer and to calculate the limited of detection (LOD). Calibration curves were obtained as follows: 4 µL of the HMI standard solution of increasing concentration was added to 1.5 mL of the filtered NP solution and the solution was left for 15 min. The standard solution with a concentration of 0.1 µm was added first, followed by higher concentrations, and the PL spectrum was measured after every addition. The net HMI concentration and the fluorescence intensity change were then plotted to determine the sensitivity and selectivity of the device. The LOD was calculated by the 3σ/K method for the linear calibration curves, where σ is the standard deviation of 8 blank measurements, as demonstrated in Fig. S1, and K is the slope of the calibration curve in the linear region [20]. For nonlinear calibration curves, the LOD was estimated by finding the concentration corresponding to 3σ in the nonlinear curve fit.

TEM was used to investigate the size and atomic arrangement of the MoS2 NPs laser treated in acetonitrile for 70 min and to observe the effects of the solvent-derived functional groups on the structure of the NPs. The NPs had an average size of approximately 7 – 8 nm, as shown in Fig. 2(a). Previous studies from our group using similar laser treatments have shown that the MoS2 NPs are a few layers thick [19]. The measured lattice spacing on one side of the particle in Fig. 2(b) is 0.27 nm, which corresponds to the (100) planes of MoS2 [19]. However, the center of the same NP shows a lattice spacing of 0.25 nm, which may correspond to carbon domains attached to the NP [21]. Other measured lattice spacings on different locations of the NP in Fig. 2(b) include 0.21 nm and 0.23 nm, which may correspond to Mo – N bonds due to nitrogen atom substitution and Mo – S bonds, respectively [22]. These different spacings are consistent with functionalization of the MoS2 by atoms from the acetonitrile.

Raman spectroscopy confirmed the solvent-derived functionalization of the NPs. The Raman spectrum of NGO in ethanol-water before laser treatment and size reduction is shown in Fig. 3(a). Clear characteristic peaks are seen at 1330, 1557, and 1614 cm−1 owing to the D band (sp2), G band (sp3), and D’ bands respectively [23,24]. Fig. 3(a) also shows the Raman spectrum of the NGO laser treated for 100 min in ethanol-water. The G and D’ peaks are split, which is attributed to the size reduction of the bulk NGO flakes into NPs and disruption in the graphitic domains due to defects and stress resulting from the addition of functional groups [25]. The effect of the water in the solvent is clear in the appearance of a 1450 cm−1 peak in the laser-treated NGO sample that can be attributed to the stretching of the hydroxyl groups attached to the carbon domains of NGO [26]. The Raman spectrum of NGO laser-treated in acetonitrile possesses different properties, as seen in Fig. 3(b), where the characteristic D and G peaks broadened until they merged, which shows the high level of defectiveness in the NGO NPs owing to the expected addition of carbon, nitrogen, hydrogen, and oxygen functional groups [27]. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the untreated MoS2 powder dispersed in acetonitrile possess in-plane and out-of-plane vibrations, as indicated by the characteristic E2 g (375.6 cm−1) and A1 g (402.5 cm−1) peaks, respectively. The Raman spectrum of the MoS2 NPs laser treated for 70 min in acetonitrile shows a slight redshift of the vibrational modes in Fig. 3(c), which may be attributable to doping by nitrogen functional groups and increased sulfur vacancies. Carbon functionalization of the MoS2 NPs is apparent from the appearance of characteristic D and G bands (Fig. 3(d)) [19].

XPS was performed to confirm the functional groups attached to the NPs and to investigate the bonds that might be responsible for chemical sensing. The C 1s spectrum of NGO laser treated in acetonitrile for 70 min is shown in Fig. 4(a). It shows distinctive peaks at 284.3, 285, 286, 287.2, and 287.7 eV, which are attributed to C–C, CO, C–N/C–OH, CC, and O–CO bonds, respectively [28,29]. The N 1s spectrum of the NGO laser treated in acetonitrile is given in Fig. 4(b) and shows three different carbon-nitrogen configurations. The pyrrolic nitrogen is the most intense peak, implying that the surface of the NPs is highly reactive due to the presence of unsatisfied bonds. The C 1s XPS spectrum of NGO treated in ethanol-water for 70 min in Fig. 4(c) shows slightly different features when compared to that of NGO treated in acetonitrile. Peaks observed at 284.4, 285.1, 286, 286.7, and 288.9 eV are assigned to C–C, CC, C–N/C–O, C–OH, and COOH bonds [28,30,31]. The O 1s spectrum of NGO in ethanol-water in Fig. 4(d) shows two characteristic peaks at 531.8 and 533.4 eV, which are attributed to C–O and CO/C–OH oxygen functionalization, respectively [32]. The Mo 3d spectrum of MoS2 treated in acetonitrile for 70 min is shown in Fig. 4(e). Characteristic MoS2 peaks are found at 229.4, 232.3, and 235 eV owing to Mo 3d3/2, Mo 3d5/2, and Mo6+ respectively [18,33]. The S 2p spectrum in Fig. 4(f) confirms the carbon functionalization of the MoS2 NPs laser-treated in acetonitrile. Carbon-sulfur bonds C–SO, C–S(O)–C, and C–SO2 are situated at 167, 168.1, and 170.1 eV, respectively [19]. Nitrogen functionalization of the MoS2 NPs laser treated in acetonitrile is confirmed by the strong N 1s signal in Fig. 4(g). The N 1s spectrum includes =N-, NH, and N+ peaks at 397.7, 399, and 399.8 eV respectively [34]. XPS surveys of the three samples are shown in Fig. S2(a-c), in which the strong XPS signals arising from the solvent-derived functional groups are clearly observed. The compositions of the characteristic peaks of all the laser-treated NPs are summarized in Tables S1-S3. Later it will be seen that some of these functional groups play a critical role in the sensing of HMI.

NGO NPs synthesized in ethanol-water have an absorption spectrum with a shoulder at 257 nm that can be attributed to the π → π * transition of the CC bond and a dominant peak at 294 nm that can be attributed to the n → π * transition of C–O–C, as seen in Fig. 5(a) [35]. The NGO NPs functionalized in acetonitrile possess different absorption properties (Fig. 5(b)) that are more similar to those of MoS2 treated in acetonitrile, which emphasizes the effect of the solvent-induced functionalization. Graphene-based NPs are expected to have multiple peaks in the range of 200 – 400 nm [28]. The main shoulders shown in Fig. 5(b) are situated at 256, 292, 320, and 370 nm and they can be attributed to π/n → π * , CC and CO, CN π → π * , and n → π * transitions [28]. The dominant peak at 413 nm and the broadening of the absorption spectrum compared to that in ethanol-water in Fig. 5(a) are attributed to amine groups formed on the surface of the NGO NPs [36]. The absorption spectrum of the MoS2 NPs treated in acetonitrile has a main peak at 400 nm and reveals a broadening of the spectrum, as shown in Fig. 5(c), consistent with the attachment of nitrogen functional groups [36].

The solvent-derived functionalization of the 2D NPs is similarly evident from their PL spectra. The NGO treated in ethanol-water was excited at 320 nm and its PL spectrum in Fig. 6(a) has a major peak at 388 nm with a long tail extended to blue emission, resembling aromatic cores and mid gap electron transitions due to oxygen functionalization [37]. On the other hand, NGO laser treated in acetonitrile was excited at 360 nm in Fig. 6(b) and it possesses a single dominant peak at 507 nm resembling π → π * (carbon domains) and n → π * transitions due to the attachment of nitrogen functional groups to the NGO NPs [23]. The MoS2 NPs laser treated in acetonitrile were excited at 280 nm and possess a main peak situated at 387 nm and a shoulder that can be seen in Fig. 6(c) at 466 – 523 nm, which is attributable to surface state emission due to nitrogen functionalization [38]. Hence it is clear that the selection of solvent dictates what functionalization occurs during the laser synthesis of the 2D nanoparticles.

The laser-treated and functionalized NPs are expected to detect toxic ions based on the high affinity of the functional groups to HMI. HMI detection was studied by examining the PL spectra of the 2D NP solutions before and after adding HMI standard solutions with different concentrations.

Fig. 7(a) shows the PL of the NGO NPs laser treated in acetonitrile being quenched as Hg2+ is added. The onset of the sensing experiment using 0.1 µm Hg2+ shows strong luminescence quenching and is followed by linear quenching behavior up to saturation at 10 µm Hg2+. Fig. 7(b) shows the PL intensity plotted versus the Hg2+ concentration. Fig. 7(c) shows the calibration curve from which a LOD of 3 nm was calculated, following the method detailed in Section 2.4. The ratio of the maximum quenched fluorescence intensity to the blank NPs fluorescence intensity is called the quenching factor (QF) and it is calculated as 1 – F/F0, where F0 is the fluorescence intensity of the NPs and F is the fluorescence intensity of the saturated quenching curve due to the addition of HMI. The QF is found to be 0.58 for Hg2+ in the NGO NPs laser treated in acetonitrile. Using the same approach, the NGO NPs laser treated in acetonitrile sensed As3+ (Fig. 7(d-f)) with a LOD of approximately 3.8 nm and a QF of 0.56. For the As3+ concentrations studied, the sensing data was best fit by a logarithmic function and the PL was not fully quenched, such that this QF represents a conservative estimate.
Cd2+ was sensed using NGO NPs laser treated in the ethanol-water mixture, as shown in Fig. 8(a). A LOD of approximately 13 nm and a QF of 0.50 were determined in Fig. 8(b-c).

The MoS2 NPs laser treated in acetonitrile were used to sense Pb2+ with a LOD of 1.8 nm and a QF of 0.64, with a strong linear correlation ( Fig. 9(a-c)). The luminescence quenching is mainly due to the decrease of the main 385 nm peak, compared to the weaker quenching observed in the nitrogen functionalization shoulder in the 450 – 550 nm range. The MoS2 NPs laser treated in acetonitrile were also used to sense Hg2+ with a LOD of approximately 10 nm and a QF of 0.87, with a logarithmic correlation (Fig. 9(d-f)). Upon the addition of 10 µm Hg2+, the luminescence is strongly quenched, mainly owing to the main MoS2 luminescence peak.

The fluorescence-quenching spectra for some of the other HMI-NP combinations did not display stable quenching; the fluorescence fluctuated as the HMI concentration was increased, as shown in Fig. S3(a-c). The fluorescence quenching spectra for several other HMI-NP combinations did not show strong quenching behavior (Fig. S4(a-c)).

The selectivity of the NP solutions towards the different heavy metals is shown in the QF bar graphs in Fig. 10. The red bars in Fig. 10 denote HMI-NP combinations for which stable quenching was observed and calibration curves could be identified. The asterisks in Fig. 10 denote the HMI-NP combinations for which reproducible quenching behavior was not observed and black bars denote combinations for which limited quenching was observed, which did not allow fitting of calibration curves. It is seen that the MoS2 NPs laser treated in acetonitrile are selective to Hg2+ and Pb2+ (Fig. 10(a)), NGO NPs laser treated in ethanol-water are selective to Cd2+ (Fig. 10(b)), and NGO NPs laser treated in acetonitrile are selective to As3+ and Hg2+ ( Fig. 10(c)). For the NP solutions that are sensitive to more than one HMI, the presence of a particular HMI can be confirmed by determining the fit of a calibration curve and matching it with the fitting expressions summarized in Table S4. Thus, selective and sensitive sensing of the four most dangerous HMI can be achieved with these laser-functionalized 2D NPs.

Different bond polarities are expected for each NP-solvent combination through charge transfer between the solvent-derived functional groups and the MoS2 or NGO NPs. Different polarities are expected to play a key role in capturing HMI selectively based on the strength of the electrostatic attraction with the HMI, as illustrated in Fig. 11 for Hg2+ sensing with NGO and MoS2. Electrostatic attraction of HMI can reduce the number of photoexcited carriers, causing PL quenching, as illustrated in Fig. 11 for a MoS2 NP that has interacted with Hg2+.

FTIR spectroscopy was used to study the role of the functional groups in the fluorescence quenching and sensing, for the NP-HMI combinations that demonstrated large QFs in Fig. 10. By examining the FTIR spectra of the laser-treated NPs in the absence and presence of the HMI, insight about the chemical affinity between the functional groups and the HMI can be obtained. The FTIR spectrum of NGO NPs laser treated in acetonitrile (black curve in Fig. 12(a)) shows characteristic C – H bending peaks in the range of 700 – 920 cm−1 and C–O and CO stretching peaks at 1035 and 1830 cm−1, respectively [39,40]. The presence of nitrogen was observed in the FTIR spectrum as N–H bending, CN stretch, and CN functional groups at 1635, 2245, and 2070 cm−1, respectively [41,42]. Hydroxyl functional groups produced a characteristic peak at 3163 cm−1, confirming the presence of oxygen functional groups, as seen in the XPS analysis [43]. Adding 10 µm of As3+ causes several changes in the FTIR spectrum including an intensity reduction of the 746 and 3163 cm −1 peaks and the broadening of 1630 cm −1 N-H peak which is attributed to the interaction of the functional groups with the arsenic ions, which could be expected to decrease the number of carriers in the NPs and lead to luminescence quenching [41]. The FTIR spectra of NGO NPs treated in acetonitrile with and without adding 50 µm Hg2+ in Fig. 12(b) show a reduction in the O – H peak at 3165 cm−1, which is attributed to the affinity of oxygen and mercury. The broadening of the 1630 cm−1 nitrogen-based peak suggests that the amine groups may facilitate interaction with the Hg2+. MoS2 NPs show a similar FTIR spectrum to NGO NPs due to laser treatment in the same solvent; however, the changes in the spectrum were different when HMI were added. Fig. 12(c) shows the FTIR spectra of MoS2 NPs laser treated in acetonitrile with and without Pb2+ added. Adding 30 µm Pb2+ resulted in an intensity reduction of the CN 2250 cm−1 peak, which is attributed to the interaction of nitrogen functional groups with Pb2+. A slight intensity reduction of the (O – H)-based 2941 and 3001 cm−1 peaks occurred, suggesting that the hydroxyl groups attached to the surface of the NPs play a role in the sensing. A significant broadening and shifting of the 1632 cm−1 N – H and 3533 and 3625 cm−1 O – H peaks also suggests that the interaction between Pb2+ and the nitrogen and oxygen functional groups attached to the NPs may reduce the photoluminescence intensity of the NPs. The FTIR spectra of MoS2 NPs laser treated in acetonitrile with and without 50 µm of Hg2+ ions added are shown in Fig. 12(d). Broadening of the O – H (3625 cm−1) peak, a reduced intensity of the CN (2245 cm−1) peak and broadening of the N – H (1627 cm−1) peak are again observed. The clear broadening of the O – H and N – H peaks may explain the fluorescence quenching through interaction of HMI with the functional groups and fewer photoexcited carriers, as mentioned earlier. It is noted that sensing Hg2+ using MoS2 treated in acetonitrile (Fig. 12(d)) resulted in different changes in the FTIR spectrum than for sensing Hg2+ using NGO treated in acetonitrile (Fig. 12(b)), despite the presence of the same functional groups. This result shows how the different polarities of the bonds between the functional groups and different NPs may lead to distinctive sensing mechanisms. A summary of the functional groups responsible for HMI sensing is shown in Table 2.

Only a weak FTIR signal could be observed for the NGO treated in ethanol-water, so PL lifetime measurements were carried out to determine the type of quenching process upon the addition of 10 µm Cd2+. The PL lifetime measurement without the addition of Cd2+ is shown as the red trace in Fig. 13 and is fit with a biexponential decay A1 e- t/τ 1 + A2 e- t/τ 2 with τ1 = 0.65 ns (A1 =98.02%) and τ2 = 2.8 ns (A2 =1.98%). The average lifetime based on the decay components and amplitudes is found to be (A1
× τ1) + (A2
× τ2) = 0.69 ns. The addition of Cd2+ (blue trace) slows the PL decay to τ1 = 0.708 ns (97.08%) and τ2 = 3.42 ns (2.92%), leading to an average lifetime of 0.79 ns. The added Cd2+ increases the PL lifetime while reducing the PL intensity, which suggests that the luminescence quenching is a static quenching process [20]. Static quenching refers to luminescence quenching due to ground state interactions between the added ions and the functional groups (O-H and/or COOH, as determined by XPS) attached to the periphery of the host material [44].

A comparison of this work with the current state of the art, including the LOD, sensing mechanism, fabrication time and method, and whether the sensing was selective, is shown in Table 3. The laser-synthesized and functionalized 2D NPs presented here can selectively sense the four most toxic HMI and are seen to compare favorably in terms of LOD and fabrication time.

In this work, a fs laser technique was used to synthesize HMI-sensing NP solutions in 70 – 100 min. The laser treatment reduced the size of the 2D materials to nanoparticles and modified them with functional groups originating from the solvents. Acetonitrile and ethanol-water solvents were used because of the high affinity of Hg2+, Pb2+, As3+, and Cd2+ to carboxylic, hydroxyl, and amine groups. The produced NPs possessed hybrid properties, as observed by UV-Vis spectroscopy, PL spectroscopy, and TEM, owing to the intrinsic properties of the NPs and the solvent-derived functional groups. Raman and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy confirmed the presence of the functional groups and the dominant functionalization with oxygen and nitrogen groups when using ethanol-water and acetonitrile, respectively.

The functionalized NPs sensed the four most toxic heavy metals with LODs of 3 nm (Hg2+), 3.8 nm (As3+), 1.8 nm (Pb2+), and 13 nm (Cd2+) in the conditions studied (room temperature and neutral pH). These LODs are more than an order of magnitude smaller than the WHO’s guidelines for chemical contaminants in drinking water, which would allow early detection of the HMI before exceeding the safe limit [46,47]. Future studies should investigate the effect of environmental conditions like pH, temperature, and water contaminants on the sensitivity and selectivity of the NPs. Laser treatment of combinations of 2D materials can also be explored to further enhance the selectivity and sensitivity of these sensors. FTIR spectroscopy analysis of the functionalized NPs before and after the addition of HMI showed that specific functional groups interact preferentially with the different HMI, enabling selective sensing via PL quenching calibration curves. Photoluminescence lifetime analysis of NGO laser treated in ethanol-water demonstrated the role of the surface carboxylic and/or hydroxyl groups in Cd2+ sensing, whereby binding of Cd ions to these groups appears to result in immediate charge transfer of some of the photoexcited charges and instantaneous PL quenching. To our knowledge, the current state of the art in sensing HMI using 2D QDs and nanosheets has not demonstrated sensing of the four most toxic HMI using one technique. In addition, most of the previous studies used QD or NP synthesis techniques that required 4 – 24 h of synthesis time. In this study, selective and sensitive sensing of HMI was achieved via the rapid fs laser treatment process.

Ahmed Shahin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis Writing – original draft. Khaled Ibrahim: Investigation, Validation. Fan Ye: Validation, Resource. Reza Karimi: Resource. Joseph Sanderson: Resources, Kevin Musselman: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was undertaken thanks in part to funding from the Canada First Research Excellence Fund (Transformative Quantum Technologies), Canada.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131576.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Guidelines for drinking-water quality: fourth edition incorporating the first addendum. Geneva: World Health Organization; 2017. Licence: CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 IGO. https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789241549950 (accessed 17 January 2022).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Musselman is an Associate Professor in the Department of Mechanical and Mechatronics Engineering at the University of Waterloo. He received his PhD in Materials Science and Metallurgy at the University of Cambridge and was a Junior Research Fellow in the Department of Physics at the University of Cambridge. He joined the University of Waterloo in 2015, where he leads the Functional Nanomaterials research group. His research program focuses on the scalable synthesis of nanomaterials and using these materials to drive the understanding and development of next-generation functional coatings and devices.

Joseph Sanderson is an Associate Professor in the Department of Physics and Astronomy at the University of Waterloo. He received his PhD in Physics at University College London (1991). Dr. Sanderson's research focuses on the study of how matter interacts with intense Femtosecond laser pulses. One of the ways which the interaction of matter with femtosecond laser pulses can be utilized is as a means of imaging some of the smallest fastest moving and most complex units of matter, molecules. He leads the Ultrafast Laser Matter Lab.

Ahmed Shahin holds a master’s degree in Nanotechnology from the University of Waterloo (2021). He is a research assistant in the Department of Mechanical and Mechatronics Engineering and a member of the Waterloo Institute of Nanotechnology at the University of Waterloo. His research focus is spectroscopy and the synthesis of 2D materials nanoparticles via femtosecond laser treatment and their utilization in chemical sensors and other applications including solar-cells stability enhancement.

Khaled Ibrahim obtained his MASc and PhD degrees at the University of Waterloo under the supervision of Prof. Kevin Mussleman and Prof. Mustafa Yavuz. The primary research focus was fabrication of novel 2D materials using laser treatment and their application in optoelectonic devices. Khaled is now a post doctoral research fellow at the University of Texas at Austin working under Prof. Jamie Warner focusing on deposition of novel epitaxial libraries of 2D materials via chemical vapor deposition.

Fan Ye is a PhD candidate under the supervision of Dr. Kevin Musselman and Dr. Joseph Sanderson in the Department of Mechanical and Mechatronics Engineering at the University of Waterloo. His PhD research focuses on the synthesis of nanomaterials by femtosecond laser irradiation for the application of photothermal cancer therapy.
